---
title: "Government Efficiency Framework and VfI Rubrics"
description: "Synthesis note mapping the Government Efficiency Framework to VfI-style rubric criteria, subcriteria, standards, and evidence prompts."
---

## Source

This synthesis uses the Government Efficiency Framework summary guide and full framework [@hm_treasury_2025_government_efficiency_framework_summary; @hm_treasury_2025_government_efficiency_framework].

It interprets the GEF through the VfI judgement architecture used in King/OPM and Verian guidance: value proposition, criteria, standards, mixed evidence, synthesis, trade-offs, and an explicit overall resource-use judgement [@king_wate_namukasa_hurrell_hansford_ward_faramarzifar_2023_opm_vfm; @king_hurrell_2024_verian_vfm].

## One-Page Summary

Direct source claims:

- The GEF defines and standardises central government efficiency reporting.
- It distinguishes technical and allocative efficiency, cash releasing and monetisable non-cash releasing savings, and efficiency from non-efficiency savings.
- It requires quality data, baselines, counterfactuals, outturns, netting of costs, no adverse impact on outcomes, sustainability, avoidance of double counting, and documentation that can withstand impartial scrutiny.
- It provides efficiency drivers, reporting expectations, maturity levels, benefits realisation guidance, risk requirements, and governance expectations.

Inferred VfI implications:

- The GEF can supply criteria and subcriteria for the efficiency and cost-effectiveness parts of a VfI rubric, but it does not supply a complete VfI method.
- VfI should provide the judgement architecture: define the value proposition, choose criteria, set standards, combine financial and non-financial evidence, handle trade-offs, and make the final resource-use judgement.
- GEF evidence standards are especially useful for distinguishing claimed savings, forecast efficiencies, delivered efficiencies, and independently defensible realised efficiencies.

Candidate rubric material:

- Use GEF definitions to test whether an efficiency claim is valid.
- Use GEF drivers to identify candidate subcriteria.
- Use GEF maturity levels and evidence requirements to design performance standards.
- Use GEF reporting and assurance expectations to build evidence prompts.

## Transparent Use Rules

| Rule | Use in VfI rubric creation | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Include | Material that defines efficiency, productivity, savings categories, evidence quality, net savings, recurrence, outcome protection, double counting, or assurance. | Directly relevant. |
| Include | Material on drivers such as procurement, workforce, demand management, digital, estates, prevention, and shared services where it helps specify candidate subcriteria. | Directly relevant as source context; rubric use is inference. |
| Use as supporting context | Reporting process, maturity levels, and governance arrangements where they inform standards for evidence strength and delivery confidence. | Relevant for standards and prompts. |
| Use as supporting context | Examples from functional areas, including property, workforce, data, digital, counter fraud, and project delivery. | Useful as examples, not universal criteria. |
| Exclude | Procedural reporting mechanics that do not affect criteria, standards, evidence, synthesis, or accountability. | Low rubric relevance. |
| Exclude | Claims of savings caused by cancellation, underspend, demand shortfall, deferral, legislation or tax changes, lower inflation, or cost shifting. | Excluded by GEF unless separate value evidence is provided. |
| Exclude from final judgement | Treating GEF financial efficiency as a complete public value assessment. | Requires wider VfI, Green Book, and Magenta Book evidence. |

## Source-to-Rubric Matrix

| Source | Strongest contribution | Candidate VfI criteria | Candidate evidence prompts |
|---|---|---|---|
| GEF summary guide | Concise definitions and criteria for efficiency savings. | Efficiency, productivity, outcome protection, evidence credibility. | Does the claim preserve or improve outputs and outcomes? Is it net, recurring, verifiable, and not double counted? |
| Full GEF chapters 1-2 | Purpose, scope, efficiency, productivity, technical efficiency, allocative efficiency. | Value proposition, efficiency, cost-effectiveness. | What is the efficiency claim, and is it technical, allocative, cash releasing, or non-cash releasing? |
| Full GEF chapter 3 | Drivers of efficiency. | Economy, efficiency, cost-effectiveness, delivery model choice. | Which driver is being used: markets, workforce, service redesign, technology, or estates? |
| Full GEF chapter 4 | Categories, criteria, baselines, net costs, cross-government effects, long-term transformation. | Cost-effectiveness, evidence quality, whole-system effects. | What baseline, counterfactual, costs to deliver, disbenefits, and cross-government impacts are evidenced? |
| Full GEF chapter 5 | Data, reporting, tracking, maturity levels, targets, public reporting. | Evidence quality, delivery confidence, accountability. | What maturity stage has the efficiency reached? Are forecast and realised savings distinguished? |
| Full GEF chapter 6 | Benefits realisation, methodology, risk, governance, assurance. | Risk, resilience, learning, accountability. | Are benefits owners, methods, risks, assurance, and escalation routes documented? |
| Green Book and Magenta Book links | Wider appraisal and evaluation context for non-monetised value. | Effectiveness, social value, distribution, mixed evidence. | What non-monetised effects and trade-offs sit outside GEF financial reporting? |

## Criteria and Subcriteria Mapping

| VfI criterion area | Candidate subcriteria from GEF | Rubric implication |
|---|---|---|
| Economy | Market management, competition, procurement, supplier costs, unit cost benchmarking, estates running costs. | Infer: strong performance should show disciplined input costs without hidden quality loss or cost shifting. |
| Efficiency | Productivity, technical efficiency, service redesign, digital automation, shared services, demand management, workforce deployment. | Infer: strong performance should show more or equal output for the same or lower input. |
| Effectiveness | No adverse impact on outcomes or strategic priorities; delivery of intended benefits. | Directly supported as a threshold: savings are not efficiency if outcomes are harmed. |
| Cost-effectiveness | Allocative efficiency, prevention, alternatives, net efficiency, costs to deliver, Green Book cost-effectiveness/cost-benefit links. | Infer: strong performance should show that the chosen resource allocation improves value relative to credible alternatives. |
| Risk and resilience | Delivery risk, realisation risk, budget control risk, escalation where plans are off track. | Infer: standards should reflect confidence that savings are deliverable, durable, and managed. |
| Accountability and assurance | Ownership, central datasets, ARAC oversight, independent scrutiny, auditability, documentation. | Infer: higher standards require transparent, reviewable claims and clear accountability. |
| Learning and capability | Data quality, benefits management, evidence reuse, digital/data process improvement, workforce capability. | Infer: rubric prompts should ask whether efficiency capability improves, not only whether a saving is booked. |

## Performance Standards: Design Prompts Only

These are prompts for standard design, not a complete rubric.

| Standard level | GEF-informed design prompt | Source status |
|---|---|---|
| Poor | Claimed saving is mainly cancellation, underspend, deferral, income increase, or reduced output; baseline and outcome protection are weak. | Inference from GEF exclusions and criteria. |
| Adequate | Efficiency opportunity is plausible and scoped, with a basic baseline, forecast, owner, and delivery plan. | Inference from maturity levels 0-2. |
| Good | Methodology is documented; forecast savings are net of costs; output/outcome impacts, risks, and double counting are actively managed. | Inference from GEF criteria, reporting, and risk sections. |
| Excellent | Realised savings are evidenced with quality data, outturns, sustained recurrence, independent assurance, benefits tracking, and no adverse outcome impact. | Inference from GEF criteria and governance expectations. |

## Evidence Prompts

For a VfI-style rubric, the GEF suggests asking:

- What is the value proposition: lower cost for the same or better output, higher output for the same cost, or reallocation to a better route to the same outcome?
- What type of efficiency is claimed: technical, allocative, cash releasing, or monetisable non-cash releasing?
- What baseline is used, and why is it credible?
- What counterfactual spending or delivery profile is used, and what assumptions drive it?
- What outturn data shows actual realised savings or productivity change?
- Are savings gross or net, and what costs to deliver have been included?
- What disbenefits, consequential costs, or impacts on other organisations have been tested?
- What evidence shows that outputs, outcomes, quality, and strategic priorities have not deteriorated?
- Are savings recurring and sustainable, rather than deferred or shifted?
- How has double counting been prevented?
- What is the maturity level of the efficiency measure?
- Who owns the benefit, who validates it, and what independent assurance has been applied?
- What risks could prevent realisation, and what escalation route exists?
- What non-monetised benefits or harms need Green Book, Magenta Book, or wider VfI evidence?

## Limits and Exclusions

The GEF is a central government efficiency and savings framework. It is not a complete VfM or VfI evaluation method. Use it to strengthen the efficiency, savings, evidence, and assurance components of a VfI rubric; use VfI guidance for the overall judgement architecture and use the Green Book and Magenta Book for wider appraisal and evaluation evidence.

All rubric implications in this note are inferences unless explicitly described as direct source claims.

## Links

Source notes:

- [Government Efficiency Framework Summary](government-efficiency-framework-summary.qmd)
- [Government Efficiency Framework](government-efficiency-framework.qmd)

Related VfI and central guidance notes:

- [Verian Value for Investment Guidance](verian-value-for-investment-guidance.qmd)
- [OPM King VfM Guidance](opm-king-vfm-guidance.qmd)
- [Magenta Book VfM Evaluation](magenta-book-vfm-evaluation.qmd)
- [Green Book VfM Guidance](green-book-vfm-guidance.qmd)

Return to the [notes overview](index.qmd).

## References
